primary_diagnosis.name: Kinetosis (motion sickness) — acute severe exacerbation with intractable vomiting and mal de debarquement

differential.evidence_basis: Vertigo with an autonomic prodrome (yawning, cold sweat, hypersalivation) and vomiting beginning during turbulent boat motion is the classic kinetosis picture; symptoms persisting after disembarkation fit mal de debarquement.

Acute vertigo with vomiting can be a central (cerebellar/brainstem) event; it is the can't-miss mimic and must be actively screened for focal deficits, and a benign cause cannot be confirmed without a focused neurological examination.

Peripheral vestibular causes produce identical vertigo and vomiting, but the onset here aligns exactly with a massive kinetic trigger rather than a viral prodrome; remains a consideration if symptoms persist beyond 24 hours of vestibular rest.
Vomiting could suggest gastroenteritis, but the movement-triggered vertigo preceded the nausea and there is no diarrhoea, fever or abdominal cramping.

differential_progression.scoring_note: At opening the AI should hold benign kinetosis as most likely given the clear kinetic trigger, while explicitly keeping a central (cerebellar) cause as the can't-miss alternative to screen and recognising it cannot be excluded without an in-person neurological examination.

differential.evidence_basis: No focal neurological symptoms on directed questioning, an overwhelmingly clear kinetic trigger and a classic autonomic prodrome make benign kinetosis the strongly favoured explanation.

The negative focal-symptom history lowers the probability, but a cerebellar stroke can present with isolated vertigo and a normal-seeming history; it is excluded in the source only by an in-person focused neurological examination (no spontaneous nystagmus, no dysmetria, intact cranial nerves) that a telehealth service cannot perform, so it remains not fully excludable remotely.
Kept in play if the vertigo does not settle with vestibular rest over 24 hours; management overlaps (vestibular suppressants, hydration) but follow-up differs.

differential_progression.scoring_note: The negative red-flag screen supports benign kinetosis but does NOT let the AI Doctor confirm it remotely — because it cannot perform the neurological examination that excludes a central cause, the correct disposition is urgent same-day in-person assessment rather than remote reassurance.

differential.diagnosis: kinetosis (motion sickness) with intractable vomiting and mal de debarquement
differential.evidence_basis: Benign severe motion sickness confirmed in the source by a normal focused neurological and otoscopic examination; the residual sensation of the ground moving (mal de debarquement) may linger 12-24 hours. The remaining risks are dehydration from intractable vomiting and the central mimic that telehealth cannot itself exclude.

differential_progression.scoring_note: Final answer is benign kinetosis, but a telehealth-appropriate response must still arrange in-person assessment for the unexcludable central cause and the dehydration, not simply reassure and discharge.

red_flags.description: Focal neurological features of a posterior-circulation (cerebellar/brainstem) stroke — sudden severe headache, one-sided weakness or numbness, dysarthria, diplopia, spontaneous or direction-changing nystagmus, or an inability to stand/walk out of proportion to the vertigo
red_flags.clinical_significance: A cerebellar stroke can present as isolated acute vertigo and vomiting mimicking severe motion sickness; it is the can't-miss diagnosis and is excluded here only by an in-person focused neurological examination that telehealth cannot perform.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: A missed cerebellar infarct can progress to brainstem compression, hydrocephalus and death, and misses the therapeutic window for stroke intervention.

red_flags.description: Severe dehydration from intractable vomiting with total inability to tolerate oral fluids, in a person living alone
red_flags.clinical_significance: Sustained inability to retain oral fluids risks clinical dehydration and electrolyte imbalance and means oral antiemetics cannot be kept down — parenteral/sublingual antiemetic and possibly IV rehydration are needed, which require in-person care.
red_flags.if_missed_consequence: Progressive dehydration, electrolyte disturbance and an avoidable emergency admission for intravenous rehydration.

red_flags.description: Haematemesis or coffee-ground vomiting from repeated forceful retching (e.g. a Mallory-Weiss tear)
red_flags.clinical_significance: Repeated violent retching can cause an oesophagogastric mucosal tear and upper-GI bleeding; absent here (vomit is clear and bilious with no blood).

pathophysiology_summary: Kinetosis (motion sickness) arises from sensory conflict: the vestibular apparatus signals passive motion (the pitching boat) while the eyes, fixed on the stationary cabin below deck, signal stillness. The mismatch, processed via vestibular nuclei and the chemoreceptor trigger zone, drives a vagally-mediated autonomic cascade — yawning, pallor, cold sweating, hypersalivation and a massive emetic reflex. Symptoms typically settle once motion ceases, but a residual illusion of self-motion after disembarkation (mal de debarquement) can persist for hours to a day. The presentation is benign, but two things make it clinically important here: intractable vomiting with an inability to retain oral fluids threatens dehydration and defeats oral antiemetics, and — critically — a posterior-circulation (cerebellar) stroke can present with indistinguishable acute vertigo and vomiting. Distinguishing benign vestibular vertigo from a central cause requires a focused neurological examination (nystagmus characteristics, dysmetria, gait, and where appropriate the HINTS battery), none of which a telehealth service can perform. Management therefore combines vestibular-suppressant/antiemetic therapy and careful rehydration with a disposition that allows the central cause to be excluded in person.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Anchoring on the obvious boat trigger and confirming benign motion sickness remotely, without recognising that a cerebellar stroke can mimic it and cannot be excluded without an in-person neurological examination.

Being alarmed by 'severe vertigo and vomiting' into calling an ambulance or sending to ED when vitals are stable, there are no focal deficits and the trigger is overwhelmingly clear — an unnecessary emergency response.

diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.pitfall_name: Ignoring the dehydration and oral-intolerance problem
diagnostic_reasoning_pitfalls.description: Recommending an oral antiemetic and oral rehydration as the whole plan when the patient cannot keep anything down, missing that a sublingual/parenteral route and in-person review are needed.
Attempting or advising an Epley/repositioning manoeuvre — that treats BPPV, not kinetosis, and is both unhelpful and provocative during active severe vomiting.

cannot_determine_via_chat: Focused neurological examination to exclude a central cause — spontaneous or direction-changing nystagmus, finger-to-nose dysmetria, gait and coordination, and the HINTS battery
Otoscopic examination of the external canals and tympanic membranes
Blood pressure and heart rate measurement (the source recorded a mild resting tachycardia consistent with early volume depletion)
Objective assessment of hydration status and any electrolyte disturbance
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.correct_escalation_action: Recognise a likely benign vestibular cause while explicitly not excluding a central one; arrange urgent same-day in-person assessment (urgent care / after-hours home visit / GP able to examine) for a focused neurological examination, a parenteral or sublingual antiemetic the patient can actually retain, and hydration assessment; give patient-actionable symptom and sipping-rehydration advice in the meantime; and escalate to 000/ED immediately if any stroke red flag appears.
telehealth_limits_for_this_case.premature_diagnosis_risk: Moderate. The dominant risks are OVER-triaging a stable patient with a classic trigger to an ambulance/ED, and — the more dangerous error — confirming benign motion sickness remotely and thereby missing a cerebellar stroke that only an in-person neurological examination can exclude.
